questions.question: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.question_raw: When the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation, it is called _______.
questions.explanation:
At equilibrium, the rate of evaporation is equal to the rate of condensation.
It may be represented by H_2O (l) ⇌ H_2O (vap)
The double half arrows indicate that the processes in both the directions are going on simultaneously. The mixture of reactants and products in the equilibrium state is called an equilibrium mixture.

questions.question:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:
questions.question_raw:
In the dynamic equilibrium stage, one of the following events takes place?
Choose the right one:

options.label: A
options.text: Rates of the forward reaction is faster.

options.label: B
options.text: The concentrations of the reactants are decreased.

options.label: C
options.text: The concentrations of the products are increased.

options.label: D
options.text: No change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.

correct_option_texts: No change in the concentrations of either of the reactants or products.
questions.explanation: There is no change in the conc of reactant and product. At dynamic equilibrium, reactants are converted to products, and products are converted to reactants at an equal and constant rate. Reactions do not necessarily—and most often do not—end up with equal concentrations. Equilibrium is the state of equal, opposite rates, not equal concentrations.

questions.question: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.question_raw: The rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmospheric pressure and 273 K. Both the processes occur simultaneously and at the same rate so that the amount of ice and water remains constant. This process is called _______.
questions.explanation: Ice and water kept in a perfectly insulated thermos flask at 273K and the atmospheric pressure are in an equilibrium state and the system shows interesting characteristic features. The mass of ice and water do not change with time and the temperature remains constant. However, the equilibrium is not static. The intense activity can be noticed at the boundary between ice and water. Molecules from the liquid water collide against ice and adhere to it and some molecules of ice escape into the liquid phase. There is no change of mass of ice and water, as the rates of transfer of molecules from ice into water and of reverse transfer from water into the ice are equal at atmosphere pressure and 273K.

questions.question: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.question_raw: If we place solid iodine in a closed vessel after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour. When equilibrium is attained, the intensity of colour will be:
questions.explanation: We place solid iodine in a closed vessel, after some time the vessel gets filled up with violet vapour and the intensity of colour increases with time. After a certain time, the intensity of colour becomes constant and at this stage, equilibrium is attained. Hence solid iodine sublimes to give iodine vapour and the iodine vapour condenses to give solid iodine. The equilibrium can be represented as I_2 (solid) $ \rightleftharpoons $ I_2 (vapour).

questions.question: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.question_raw: The boiling point of water at atmospheric pressure is ________.
questions.explanation: Water and water vapour are in equilibrium position at atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar) and at 100°C in a closed vessel. The boiling point of water is 100°C at 1.013 bar pressure. For any pure liquid at one atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar), the temperature at which the liquid and vapours are at equilibrium is called the normal boiling point of the liquid. The boiling point of the liquid depends on the atmospheric pressure.

questions.question: _______ states that "the mass of a gas dissolved in a given mass of a solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent."
questions.question_raw: _______ states that "the mass of a gas dissolved in a given mass of a solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent."
questions.explanation: This equilibrium is governed by Henry’s law, which states that the mass of a gas dissolved in a given mass of a solvent at any temperature is proportional to the pressure of the gas above the solvent. This amount decreases with an increase in temperature.

questions.question: When the soda bottle is opened, some of the dissolved carbon dioxide gas escapes because of:
questions.question_raw: When the soda bottle is opened, some of the dissolved carbon dioxide gas escapes because of:

options.label: A
options.text: difference in solubility of carbon dioxide at different pressures.

options.label: B
options.text: some of the undissolved carbon dioxide gas in the soda bottle.

options.label: C
options.text: difference in solubility of carbon dioxide at different temperatures.

options.label: D
options.text: to reach a new equilibrium condition required for the higher pressure.

correct_option_texts: difference in solubility of carbon dioxide at different pressures.
questions.explanation: Soda water, like other carbonated beverages, contains carbon dioxide that has dissolved under pressure. When the pressure is released by opening the soda container, the liquid cannot hold as much carbon dioxide, so the excess bubbles out of the solution. If the soda is left open, additional carbon dioxide will slowly escape into the air. Under warm conditions, the carbon dioxide leaves the solution faster.

questions.question_raw: The dynamic nature of chemical equilibrium can be demonstrated in the synthesis of ammonia by Haber's process. Choose the appropriate option given below:

options.label: C
options.text: by maintaining high temperature and low pressure

questions.explanation: The use of isotope (deuterium) in the formation of ammonia clearly indicates that chemical reactions reach a state of dynamic equilibrium in which the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal and there is no net change in composition.

questions.question_raw: At a given temperature, equilibrium is possible only in a system which is a:
questions.explanation: Equilibrium is possible only in a closed system at a given temperature for physical processes. When equilibrium is attained for a physical process, it is characterized by the constant value of one of its parameters at a given temperature.

questions.question: The concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, ${{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}} = $ $\frac{{\left[ C \right]\left[ D \right]}}{{\left[ A \right]\left[ B \right]}}$ Where Kc is called as ________.

questions.explanation:
Let us consider a general reversible reaction:
A + B $ \rightleftharpoons $ C + D
where A and B are the reactants, C and D are the products in the balanced chemical equation.
On the basis of experimental studies of many reversible reactions, opposed in 1864 that the concentrations in an equilibrium mixture are related by the following equilibrium equation, K_c = [C] [D] /[A] [B]
Where K_c is the equilibrium constant and the expression on the right side is called the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: A particular ratio of product to reactant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage is called ________.
questions.question_raw: A particular ratio of product to reactant helps in predicting the direction in which a given reaction will proceed at any stage is called ________.
questions.explanation:
The reaction quotient Q. For a general reaction: aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD
Qc = [C]^c [D]^d / [A]^a [B]^b 
Then, If Q_c > Kc,
the reaction will proceed in the direction of reactants (reverse reaction).
If Q_c < K_c, the reaction will proceed in the direction of the products (forward reaction).
If Q_c = K_c, the reaction mixture is already at equilibrium.

questions.question: The value of K_c for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.question_raw: The value of Kc for a reaction is directly related to the thermodynamics of the reaction and in particular with:
questions.explanation:
A mathematical expression of this thermodynamic view of equilibrium can be described by the following equation: $\Delta$G = $\Delta$G^0 + RT ln Q
where G^0 is standard Gibbs energy.
At equilibrium, when $\Delta$G = 0 and Q = K_c,
$\Delta$G = $\Delta$G^0 + RT ln K = 0
$\Delta$G^0 = – RT lnK (7.22)
lnK = – $\Delta$G^0 / RT
Taking antilog of both sides, we get,
K = e^– $\Delta$G0 / RT

questions.question: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:
questions.question_raw: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction for the conversion of reactants to products by:

options.label: B
options.text: lowering the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture

options.label: C
options.text: increasing the activation energy for the forward reaction

options.label: D
options.text: making available a new low energy pathway

correct_option_texts: making available a new low energy pathway
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases the rate of the chemical reaction by making available a new low energy pathway for the conversion of reactants to products. It increases the rate of forward and reverse reactions that pass through the same transition state and does not affect equilibrium. Catalyst lowers the activation energy for the forward and reverse reactions by exactly the same amount. Catalyst does not affect the equilibrium composition of a reaction mixture. It does not appear in the balanced chemical equation or in the equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question: A bare proton, H^+ is very reactive and cannot exist freely in aqueous solutions. Thus, it bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule to give:
questions.question_raw: A bare proton, H+ is very reactive and cannot exist freely in aqueous solutions. Thus, it bonds to the oxygen atom of a solvent water molecule to give:
questions.explanation:
Hydrogen ion by itself is a bare proton with a very small size (~10–15 m radius) and intense electric field, binds itself with the water molecule at one of the two available lone pairs on it giving H_3O^+. This species has been detected in many compounds (e.g., H_3O^+Cl ^– ) in the solid-state.
In aqueous solution, the hydronium ion is further hydrated to give species like ${H_5}O_2^ + $.

questions.question: Hydronium ion concentration in molarity is more conveniently expressed on a logarithmic scale known as the _______.
questions.question_raw: Hydronium ion concentration in molarity is more conveniently expressed on a logarithmic scale known as the _______.
questions.explanation:
Hydronium ion concentration in molarity is more conveniently expressed on a logarithmic scale known as the pH scale. The pH of a solution is defined as the negative logarithm to base 10 of the activity (H^+) of hydrogen ion. In dilute solutions (< 0.01 M), the activity of hydrogen ion (H^+) is equal in magnitude to molarity represented by [H^+].
From the definition of pH= – log H^+= – log [H^+] / mol L^–1.
Thus, an acidic solution of HCl (10^-2 M) will have a pH = 2.
Similarly, a basic solution of NaOH having [OH^​​​​​-] =10^-4 M and [H_3O^+] = 10^-10 M will have a pH = 10.
At 25 °C, pure water has a concentration of hydrogen ions, [H^+] = 10^-7M. Hence, the pH of pure water is given as: pH = –log(10^–7) = 7.

questions.question: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.question_raw: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali is called ________.
questions.explanation: The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali are called Buffer Solutions. Buffer solutions of known pH can be prepared from the knowledge of pKa of the acid or pK b of base and by controlling the ratio of the salt and acid or salt and base. A mixture of acetic acid and sodium acetate acts as a buffer solution around pH 4.75 and a mixture of ammonium chloride and ammonium hydroxide acts as a buffer around pH 9.25.

K_a_1, K_a2 and K_a3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H_2S $ \rightleftharpoons $ H^+ + HS^-
HS^- $ \rightleftharpoons $ H^+ + S^2-
H_2S $ \rightleftharpoons $ 2H^+ + S^2-
The correct relationship between K_a1, K_a2 and K_a3 is:
questions.question_raw:
Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 are the respective ionisation constants for the following reactions:
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + HS-
HS- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + S2-
H2S {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} 2H+ + S2-
The correct relationship between Ka1, Ka2 and Ka3 is:
questions.explanation:
The ionization reactions for example for a dibasic acid H_2X are represented by the equations:
H_2X(aq) ⇌ H^+ (aq) + HX^– (aq)
HX^– (aq) ⇌ H^+ (aq) + X^2– (aq)
the corresponding equilibrium constants are given below:$K_{a_1}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack HX^–\rbrack}{\lbrack H_2X\rbrack}$and $K_{a_2}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack X^{2–}\rbrack}{\lbrack HX^-\rbrack}$
Here, Ka_1 and Ka_2 are called the first and second ionization constants respectively of the acid H_2 X.
For ${\;{H_2}S\; \rightleftharpoons \;2{H^ + }\; + \;{S^{2 - }}}$
${{K_{{a_3}}} = \frac{{{{[{H^ + }]}^2}[{X^2}]}}{{[{H_2}X]}}}$
It is clear that, $K_{a_1}\times K_{a_2}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack HX^–\rbrack}{\lbrack H_2X\rbrack}\times\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack\lbrack X^{2–}\rbrack}{\lbrack HX^-\rbrack}=\frac{\lbrack H^+\rbrack^2\lbrack X^{2–}\rbrack}{\lbrack H_2X\rbrack}\;=K_{a_3}$
Therefore, $\;K_{a_3}=K_{a_1}\times\;K_{a_2}$

questions.question: H_2S is passed into one dm^3 of a solution containing 0.1 mole of Zn^2+ and 0.01 mole of Cu^2+ till the sulphide ion concentration reaches 8.1$\times$10^-19 moles. Which one of the following statements is true? [K_sp of ZnS and CuS are 3 $\times$ 10^-22 and 8 $\times$ 10^-36 respectively]
questions.question_raw: H2S is passed into one dm3 of a solution containing 0.1 mole of Zn2+ and 0.01 mole of Cu2+ till the sulphide ion concentration reaches 8.1{tex}\times{/tex}10-19 moles. Which one of the following statements is true? [Ksp of ZnS and CuS are 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-22 and 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-36 respectively]
questions.explanation:
Precipitation occurs only when the ionic product exceeds the value of the solubility product.
1 dm^3 of the solution containing 0.1 mole of Zn^2+, 0.01 mole of Cu^2+, and 8.1 $\times$ 10^-39 mole of S^2-.
Let us calculate the ionic product in each case.
Ionic product of ZnS = [Zn^2+] [S^2-]
0.1 $ \times$ 8.1 $ \times$ 10^-19 = 8.1 $ \times$ 10^-20
K_sp of ZnS = 3 $ \times$ 10^-22
Here, Ionic Product > K_sp
Ionic Product of CuS = [Cu^2+] [S^2-]
= 0.01 $ \times$ 8.1 $ \times$ 10 ^- 19 = 8.1 $ \times$ 10^-21
But it has K_w = 8 $ \times$ 10^-36
Since, Ionic product > K_sp
As both ZnS and CuS have less K_sp value than their ionic product so ZnS and CuS both get precipitated.

questions.question_raw: pH of a solution of a strong acid is 5.0. What will be the pH of the solution obtained after diluting the given solution a 100 times?
questions.explanation:
pH=5 means the solution is acidic. On dilution, it will remain acidic. However, acidic character decreases so much that it is almost neutral (pH=5 means [H^+] = 10^-5M). After dilution, [H^+] = $\frac{10^{-5}}{100}=10^{-7}M$ ; [H^+] from H_2O cannot be neglected.
Hence total [H^+] = 10^-7 + 10^-7 = 2 $\times$ 10^-7 M
$\Rightarrow$ pH=-log(2 $\times$ 10^-7 ) = 7-0.3010=6.699

${\Delta _{rxn}}H = (\Delta {H_{product}} - \Delta {H_{reac\tan t}})$
= -393.5 - 635.1 + 1206.9 = +ve

questions.explanation:
Pressure will increase in the forwarded reaction and the number of moles of the products increase.
 Pressure will increase in the backward reaction and the number of moles of the products decrease.

questions.question: One of the reaction that takes place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO_2. FeO(s) + CO (g) $\rightarrow$ Fe (s) + CO_2 (g) K_p = 0.265 at 1050 K. What are the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO_2 at 1050 K if the initial partial pressures are: P_co = 1.4 atm and $P_{CO_2}$ = 0.80 atm?
questions.question_raw: One of the reaction that takes place in producing steel from iron ore is the reduction of iron (II) oxide by carbon monoxide to give iron metal and CO2. FeO(s) + CO (g) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Fe (s) + CO2 (g) Kp = 0.265 at 1050 K. What are the equilibrium partial pressures of CO and CO2 at 1050 K if the initial partial pressures are: Pco = 1.4 atm and {tex}P_{CO_2}{/tex} = 0.80 atm?
questions.explanation:
For the given reaction; $\mathop {FeO + CO(g)}\limits_{1.4atm} = \mathop {Fe(s) + C{O_2}(g)}\limits_{0.80atm} $
$Q_p=\frac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{CO}}=\frac{0.80}{1.4}=\;0.571$
It is given that K_p = 0.265
Since Q_P > K_p
The reaction will proceed in the backward reaction.
Therefore, we can say that the pressure of CO will increase while the pressure of CO_2 will decrease.
Now, let the increase in pressure of CO = decrease in pressure of CO_2 be P.
Then we can write
${{K_p} = \frac{{{P_{C{O_2}}}}}{{{P_{CO}}}} = \frac{{0.80 - P}}{{1.4 + P}}}$
${ \Rightarrow 0.265 = \frac{{0.80 - P}}{{1.4 + P}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ 0.265 (1.4 + P) = 0.80 - P
$\Rightarrow$ 0.371 + 0.265P = 0.80 - P
$\Rightarrow$ 1.265P = 0.429 $\Rightarrow$ p = 0.339atm
Therefore, equilibrium partial pressure of CO_2 = 0.80 - 0.339= 0.461 atm
Also, equilibrium partial pressure of CO = 1.4 + 0.339 = 1.739 atm

questions.question: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 $\times$ 10^-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H_3O^+,F^- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.question_raw: The ionization constant of HF is 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4. Calculate the degree of dissociation of HF in its 0.02 M solution. The concentrations of all species present i.e. H3O+,F- and HF in the solution, and its pH respectively are.
questions.explanation:
HF $\rightleftharpoons$ H^+ + F^-
Initial Concentration 1 0 0
Equilibrium concentration C(1 - $\alpha$) C $\alpha$ C $\alpha$
Now, ${{K_a}\; = \frac{{\left[ {{H^ + }} \right]\left[ {{F^ - }} \right]}}{{\left[ {HF} \right]}} = \frac{{\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]\left[ {\;C\alpha } \right]}}{{\left[ {C(1 - \alpha )\;} \right]}} = \frac{{\;C{\alpha ^2}}}{{(1 - \alpha )\;}} \approx C{\alpha ^2}}$ (Since $\alpha$ is less (1 - $\alpha$ ) can be written as 1
${ \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt {\frac{{{K_a}\;}}{C}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{0.02}}} = 12.65 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}$
[H_3O^+] = C$\alpha$ =0.02 $\times$ 0.1265 = 2.53 $\times$ 10^-3
[F^-] = C$\alpha$ = 0.02 $\times$ 0.1265 = 2.53 $\times$10^-3
[HF]= C(1-$\alpha$) = 0.02 $\times$ (1-0.1265) = 17.6 $\times$ 10^-3
Now,
pH= - log [H_3O^+] = - log (2.53 $\times$ 10^-3) = 3 - 0.4031 = 2.5969

questions.question: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.question_raw: Assuming complete dissociation, calculate the pH of 0.002 M KOH solution.
questions.explanation:
KOH $\to$ K^+ + OH^- (As KOH undergoes complete ionization)
$\Rightarrow$ [OH^-] = [KOH] = 0.02
Now, K_w = [H^+] [OH^-]${ \Rightarrow \left[ {{H^ + }} \right] = \frac{{\;{K_w}\;}}{{\left[ {O{H^ - }} \right]}} = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{0.02}} = \;5 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ pH of KOH = - log [5 $\times$ 10^-12] = 12 - log 5 = 12 - 0.699 $\approx$ 11.31
